prompt: Mr. Madison, you are writing in the National Gazette about property rights. You seem to use the word 'property' in an unusually broad sense. What do you mean by it?
ground_truth_signal: Madison articulated an expansive definition: 'In its larger and juster meaning, it embraces every thing to which a man may attach a value and have a right... a man has a property in his opinions and the free communication of them. He has a property of peculiar value in his religious opinions, and in the profession and practice dictated by them. He has a property very dear to him in the safety and liberty of his person... In a word, as a man is said to have a right to his property, he may be equally said to have a property in his rights.' He further stated: 'Government is instituted to protect property of every sort... that alone is a just government, which impartially secures to every man, whatever is his own.' And: 'Conscience is the most sacred of all property; other property depending in part on positive law, the exercise of that, being a natural and unalienable right.' Source: 'Property,' National Gazette, March 27, 1792.
response:
I am pleased you have attended to this distinction, for it touches upon a fundamental principle that I fear is too little understood among our countrymen. When I employ the term "property" in my essays for the National Gazette, I intend something far more comprehensive than the common usage, which confines itself to a man's material possessions — his lands, his goods, his chattels.

In its larger and juster meaning, property embraces every thing to which a man may attach a value and have a right. A man has property in his opinions and the free communication of them. He has property in the safety and liberty of his person. He has an equal property in the free use of his faculties and free choice of the objects on which to employ them. In a word, as a man is said to have a right to his property, he may be equally said to have a property in his rights.

This broader conception is not merely philosophical speculation — it rests upon the very foundation of just government. Where an excess of power prevails, property of no sort is duly respected. No man is safe in his opinions, his person, his faculties, or his possessions. Where there is an excess of liberty, the effect is the same, though from an opposite cause. Government is instituted to protect property of every sort, and where the government itself becomes the instrument of violating that property, it has perverted its essential purpose.

I have observed that those who hold the narrower view — who speak only of property in external things — often become the instruments of injustice toward property in its broader sense. They would protect a man's estate while destroying his liberty of conscience. They would secure his goods while denying him the fruits of his industry or the free exercise of his talents. This is to mistake the part for the whole, the means for the end.

The man who is secure in his property, properly understood, is secure in his person, his liberty, and his pursuit of happiness. He who would be secure in these must be equally vigilant in defending the property rights of others, for the principle that protects one protects all. This is why I have written that a just security to property is not afforded by that government under which unequal taxes oppress one species of property and reward another species — for such a government violates the property which individuals have in their faculties, by preventing the free exercise of those faculties for the acquisition of property.

The distinction is not merely academic. It strikes at the heart of what we mean by republican government and the rights of man.
